primary_factors: No accumulating snow signal (0") and temperatures above freezing in the provided forecast window; precip type is rain
No extreme cold/wind chill trigger (no credible source shows ≤ -20°F during bus hours)
Infrastructure/roads: no plowing expected; roads generally passable—main risks are wet pavement and patchy fog
Flood Warning in the Grand River/Comstock Park area could create isolated standing-water issues on a few low-lying routes (not typically district-wide closure)
News intel: no neighboring-district closure announcements and no community buzz suggesting an impending closure
decision_rationale:
All experts converge on a non-snow event: the meteorology shows rain (not snow), temperatures mainly above freezing, and no meaningful wind-chill hazard. The only notable hazard is hydrology/visibility: an active Flood Warning near Comstock Park plus wet roads and possible fog could create isolated trouble spots (low-lying rural roads, underpasses, and a few bus-route detours), but that historically does not drive district-wide closure in West Michigan.

The one meaningful conflict was the Safety Assessment’s earlier ‘moderate ice risk’ and a 05:30–09:00 worst window; on follow-up, the safety analyst explicitly retracted that as an overreach given the provided dataset lacks Monday 3/23 4–8 AM subfreezing evidence and Sunday-night temps in the supplied hours stay above 32°F. News intelligence also shows no confirmed neighboring district closures/delays and no social/media ‘closure buzz,’ which strongly argues against a domino-effect closure.

Net: closure probability stays very low (≈5%). The more plausible operational outcome, if anything changes, would be a localized/short delay due to fog or a few flooded segments—not a snow day.

collaboration.summary: Agents reached consensus after 1 round(s) with 4.0% spread. Average probability: 5%.
